speaker: Host
text_en: How has the creator of Ruby on Rails changed how we build software now with AI agents? David Heinemeyer Hanson often approaches DHH, creator of Ruby on Rails, Omachi, and is the co-founder of 37 Signals.
text: AI agents 出现之后，Ruby on Rails 的创造者是怎么改变我们如今构建软件的方式的？David Heinemeyer Hanson，大家通常叫他 DHH，是 Ruby on Rails 和 Omachi 的创始人，也是 37 Signals 的联合创始人。

speaker: Host
text_en: He bashed capabilities of AI coding tools on LexReason's podcast six months ago. Then, over the course of a few weeks over the winter break, he did a 180 turn and went AI first on everything.
text: 六个月前，他还在 LexReason 的播客里猛批 AI 编程工具的能力。结果到了寒假那几周里，他来了个 180 度大转弯，几乎所有事情都开始 AI 优先了。

speaker: Host
text_en: In today's conversation we cover how David and his team at 37signals builds software today and how AI tools are making them more ambitious than ever before.
text: 今天这期对话里，我们会聊 David 和他在 37signals 的团队现在是怎么做软件的，以及 AI 工具如何让他们比以往任何时候都更有野心。

speaker: Host
text_en: Why Ruby on Rails and Linux could become even more popular than they are today as they are both well suited working with AI agents.
text: 我们还会聊，为什么 Ruby on Rails 和 Linux 可能会比今天更受欢迎，因为它们都非常适合和 AI agents 一起工作。

speaker: Host
text_en: Why taste and beautiful software are becoming more important and why bold standout designers and engineers who care about the craft could become more in demand. and many more.
text: 为什么审美和优美的软件变得更重要了，以及为什么那些大胆、有辨识度、又真正重视手艺的设计师和工程师，可能会变得更抢手。还有很多别的话题。

speaker: Host
text_en: If you're interested in what one of the most experienced builders in the tech industry thinks about the practical utility of AI tools and how these tools could impact software engineers who care about the craft, then this episode is for you.
text: 如果你想知道科技行业里最资深的构建者之一，怎么看 AI 工具的实际价值，以及这些工具会怎样影响那些真正重视手艺的软件工程师，那这期节目就是给你的。

speaker: Host
text_en: This episode is presented by Statsig, the unified platform for flags, analytics experiments, and more. Check out the show notes to learn more about them and our other season sponsor, Sonar and WorkOS. David, it's awesome to have you here.
text: 本期节目由 Statsig 呈现，它是一个集 flags、分析、实验等功能于一体的统一平台。想进一步了解他们，以及我们本季的另外两家赞助商 Sonar 和 WorkOS，可以查看节目备注。David，很高兴请到你。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Thanks for having me. Thanks for coming, I should actually say. You're in Copenhagen. That's my city of choice at the moment. It's a beautiful city. It's got so much going for it.
text: 谢谢邀请我来。其实应该说，谢谢你过来才对。你现在在 Copenhagen。那是我这阵子最想待的城市。那真是一座美丽的城市，优点特别多。

speaker: Host
text_en: And so what have you been up to?
text: 那你最近都在忙些什么？

text_en: I'm always building stuff. I have been building stuff for a good damn three decades now on the internet. I got started back in 94, I think it was, when I first got exposed to it and basically just never stopped.
text: 我一直都在做东西。过去整整三十来年，我都在互联网上不停地做各种东西。我大概是 94 年第一次接触到互联网，从那之后基本就没停过。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And in the past six months, I've been building a variety of things. One of them is a new Linux distribution called Umachi. I switched to Linux about a little over two years ago, I think now. First spent some time on Ubuntu,
text: 过去这六个月里，我做了不少东西。其中一个是新的 Linux 发行版，叫 Umachi。我大概是两年多前转到 Linux 的。最开始先用了段时间 Ubuntu，

speaker: Guest
text_en: having fun with that and then realizing I actually wanted to make my own system from scratch, building it on top of Arch and Hyperland. So put a lot of time into Machi, got started as a summer project in between racing at the 24 Hours of Le Mans.
text: 玩得挺开心，后来又意识到，其实我想从零开始做一套自己的系统，基于 Arch 和 Hyperland 来构建。所以我在 Machi 上投入了很多时间，它最初是我在参加勒芒 24 小时耐力赛间隙做的一个暑期项目。

speaker: Guest
text_en: There's a lot of downtime in that week. So I just started hacking on it and it really took off very quickly thereafter.
text: 那一周其实有很多空档时间，所以我就开始动手折腾，之后它很快就发展起来了。

text: 这一路真的很受鼓舞。即便是在 Linux 发行版这么拥挤的市场里，外面大概已经有 7000 种不同的发行版了，

text_en: some of them with long pedigrees and many of them even based on sort of kind of similar vibes to some extent. There's room for something new and it's a great reminder that.
text: 有些历史非常悠久，很多在某种程度上甚至连气质都挺相似的。但依然有新东西的空间，这也很好地提醒了我一点。

speaker: Guest
text_en: built the perfect computer system for me and saw exactly the same thing I've ever seen whenever I build something that really just hits the spot for me personally.
text: 我给自己打造过一套堪称完美的计算机系统，然后我发现了一件和我每次做出真正特别对自己胃口的东西时一模一样的事。

speaker: Guest
text_en: There are thousands of others just like me or close enough to what I like that they find the same pleasure and joy in it, whether it was Ruby on Rails, Kamal getting out of the cloud, any of these things. It's the same syndrome.
text: 世界上还有成千上万的人和我一样，或者至少口味足够接近，所以他们也会从这些东西里获得同样的满足和快乐。不管是 Ruby on Rails、Kamal、离开云，还是这些事情里的任何一个，都是同一种症状。

speaker: Host
text_en: Yeah, with Rails, you were literally scratching your own itch, you were just building your own components and then open sourcing them. Is that how it started?
text: 对，做 Rails 的时候，你其实就是在解决自己的痛点，你只是先做自己的组件，然后把它们开源了。事情一开始就是这样吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Basically, I picked up Ruby in the early 2000s and really put it to the test in 2003 when we started building.
text: 基本上是这样。我在 2000 年代初开始接触 Ruby，到了 2003 年我们开始做产品时，才算是真正拿它上强度。

speaker: Guest
text_en: building base camp and I did not have a mandate of what to use to build it prior to that I've been working for a lot of client projects that would say well we're building this in PHP because we have someone who knows that so this is what you have to use and then we were building our own system we're building base camp and I was free to choose so I chose Ruby
text: 开始做 Basecamp 的时候，我事先并没有被规定必须用什么来开发。在那之前，我做过很多客户项目，他们会说，嗯，我们这个要用 PHP 来做，因为我们有人懂这个，所以你就得用它。后来我们开始做自己的系统，做 Basecamp，我可以自由选择，所以我选了 Ruby。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And at the time, Ruby didn't have any tooling or not very much when it came to web applications. So I had to build it all myself. And that turned into Ruby on Rails, which is still going strong. I'm still very heavily involved with that.
text: 而在当时，Ruby 在 Web 应用这块几乎没什么工具，或者说工具非常少。所以我只能自己把整套东西做出来，后来这就变成了 Ruby on Rails。它现在依然发展得很好，我到现在也还深度参与其中。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think in some ways, Ruby on Rails is having a little bit of a renaissance now that it is one of the most token efficient ways of building web apps. It's ideally suited for the agent workflows we're dealing with now.
text: 我觉得从某种意义上说，Ruby on Rails 现在有点复兴的感觉，因为它是构建 Web 应用时 token 效率最高的方式之一。它和我们现在面对的 agent 工作流简直是天然契合。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Now, we'll see how long that lasts. Maybe all the agents are going to be writing machine code or assembler in about five minutes. So maybe that comes to an end. But for the moment,
text: 当然，这种情况能持续多久还不好说。没准再过五分钟，所有 agent 就都开始写机器码或者汇编了，那这波可能也就结束了。不过至少眼下是这样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: token efficiency still matters and it still matters whether the agents produce code that humans are able to read and verify that may also come to an end at some point.
text: token 效率依然很重要，agent 产出的代码人类是否能看懂、能验证也依然很重要，不过这件事在某个时间点也可能会走到尽头。

speaker: Guest

text: 就目前来说，看到这类我为了满足自己需求而做的项目，能引起更大社区的共鸣，然后大家纷纷出现、想要帮忙，这一路真的很有意思。比如说 Umachi，它到现在也才存在了，怎么说呢，刚刚超过六个月。

speaker: Guest

text: 我们已经有，差不多 400 位贡献者给这个发行版提交过代码改动。除此之外，还有几万人安装了它，并把它当作自己的日常主力系统。所以我一直很喜欢那种发现新东西的感觉，

speaker: Guest
text_en: novel, and inspiring like Ruby.
text: 那种像 Ruby 一样新鲜、独特又让人受启发的东西。

text: 或者说，把一个从 91 年就存在的操作系统说成是“发现”，听起来有点怪。但对很多人来说，Linux 现在确实就是那种新发现，因为他们以前并没有在自己的个人电脑上用过它。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So they're seeing it for the first time. And for me to help a new cohort of Linux users and hopefully even enthusiasts come to be because I'm flattening the curve a little bit, I'm making it easier to get started, I'm making the default installation just look amazing.
text: 所以他们是第一次接触它。而对我来说，如果因为我把门槛稍微拉平了一点，让入门变得更容易，让默认安装出来的效果看起来就很惊艳，从而帮助新一批 Linux 用户，甚至是爱好者出现，那会让我特别开心。

text: 这样一来，他们不会觉得自己非得花上 100 个小时去折腾系统才能开始用，这真的很有意思。当然，另一件同样有意思的事是，这两样东西——Ruby、Rails，还有 Amachii——都不只是业余项目。我很喜欢业余项目，以后也一直会做，但我也喜欢把它们应用到商业里。所以在 37signals，我们基于 Ruby 和 Rails 建立起了一整套持续了 20 多年的业务，而现在我们也在开发者的大多数机器上运行 Linux。
